questions.question: What is the orchestration pressure that justifies a Service Layer?

options.A: Multiple domain objects share a single database table
options.B: The same use case sequence is needed in multiple entry points and would otherwise scatter
options.C: The application has too many classes for a single developer to understand
options.D: Domain objects are too simple to hold any behavior

questions.explanation: Service Layer is justified when the same multi-step operation (validate, load, check, update, notify) must be coordinated consistently across multiple entry points. A is a persistence concern, C is a complexity concern unrelated to patterns, and D describes a symptom of anemic domain model, not a Service Layer justification.

questions.question: Where does Service Layer stop, and what is the failure mode when it expands beyond its boundary?

options.A: It stops at the database; failure mode is too many SQL queries
options.B: It stops at orchestration; failure mode is absorbing domain rules and creating an anemic domain model
options.C: It stops at the UI; failure mode is coupling to presentation concerns
options.D: It stops when it reaches 500 lines; failure mode is a God class

questions.explanation: Service Layer owns orchestration (sequencing, coordination). Business rules belong in domain objects. When Service Layer absorbs rules, domain objects become data bags — the anemic domain model. A, C, D describe different but incorrect boundaries.

questions.question: A method checks `if user is None: return 'Guest'` in 15 separate places. What does the Special Case pattern replace these checks with?

options.A: A centralized null check utility function called once
options.B: A GuestUser object that implements the same interface as User with default behavior
options.C: A database default value that prevents null users from being created
options.D: A validation layer that rejects null inputs before they reach the method

questions.explanation: Special Case replaces repeated null checks with a polymorphic object (GuestUser) that provides correct default behavior through the same interface — callers no longer branch. A centralizes but doesn't eliminate the check. C and D prevent the case from arising rather than handling it with the pattern.

questions.question: What edge-case pressure justifies Special Case (Null Object) over a simple null check?

options.A: The null check logic is complex and involves more than one condition
options.B: The same edge condition is checked in many places and each check must be kept consistent
options.C: The team wants to avoid using null values in Python
options.D: The object being checked is an external dependency

questions.explanation: The pressure is scattered, repeated edge-case handling that must stay consistent. When the same null/sentinel check appears in many places, any policy change requires touching all of them. Special Case localizes the policy once. A describes complexity, not recurrence. C is a style preference. D is a boundary issue, not a Special Case trigger.

questions.question: Claim C3 states structural patterns are most valuable at unstable or mismatched boundaries. Which of the following best applies this claim?

options.A: Adding a facade around a stable, well-documented internal library the team controls
options.B: Adding an adapter at the boundary between the domain and a third-party API whose response format changes with each version
options.C: Using a service layer to reduce the number of classes in the codebase
options.D: Applying data mapper to a project with a single SQLite table and no domain behavior

questions.explanation: C3 is about boundary instability and mismatch. A third-party API with changing response formats is the clearest example of an unstable, mismatched boundary where structural isolation pays. A describes a stable boundary. C is not a structural-pattern justification. D applies data mapper before domain complexity warrants it.

questions.question: A system has both orchestration pressure (repeated use-case sequences) and edge-condition pressure (null user in 10 places). How should these two pressures be addressed?

options.A: Pick the most pressing one and use one pattern for both
options.B: Address each with its own pattern: Service Layer for orchestration, Special Case for edge conditions
options.C: Use Service Layer and add null-handling logic inside the service methods
options.D: Use Special Case for everything and skip the Service Layer

questions.explanation: Coherent composition addresses each distinct pressure with its own pattern. Service Layer owns orchestration; Special Case owns the null policy. Merging them (A, C) or picking one (D) leaves one pressure unaddressed or mixes concerns.

questions.question: How do you decide between Service Layer and Registry when both seem like potential responses to an access boundary problem?

options.A: Service Layer for problems with more than three steps; Registry for simpler problems
options.B: Service Layer for orchestrating a sequence of operations; Registry for locating a shared object
options.C: Registry is always preferred because it requires less code
options.D: Service Layer is always preferred because it provides better testability

questions.explanation: The patterns address different pressures: Service Layer = use-case coordination (sequencing operations), Registry = lookup (finding a shared object without threading it through call trees). Pressure maps to pattern. A uses complexity as a proxy — incorrect. C and D are blanket preferences, not pressure-based reasoning.

questions.question: Explain why Special Case (Null Object) is described as 'localizing policy' and null checks are described as 'scattering policy.' Give an example showing the difference.

required_points: Null check: each call site is responsible for deciding what to do with a null — the policy lives at the point of use and can drift between sites
Special Case: the policy is inside the GuestUser (or similar) object — it is called once when the degenerate object is created and applied consistently wherever the object is used
Example: user.display_name() returns 'Guest' from GuestUser without the caller checking for null — the policy is local to the object, not scattered across callers
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any domain example showing the localization
answer_key.full_credit: Correct policy-localization vs. policy-scattering distinction with concrete example
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct principle without example, or example without the localization explanation

questions.question: A Service Layer method contains this logic: `if order.total > 1000 and order.customer.tier == 'gold': apply_discount(order, 0.15)`. Should this stay in the service or move to the domain? Justify your answer using what you know about where business rules belong.

required_points: This is a business rule (discount eligibility based on order total and customer tier) — it belongs in the domain
Service Layer should orchestrate (call domain.apply_discount_if_eligible()) not contain the rule
If the rule stays in the service, any other workflow needing the same discount logic must duplicate it or call this service — violating encapsulation
Domain location: e.g., Order.apply_eligible_discount() or a domain service that knows both Order and Customer
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May place the rule on Order or on a domain service — either is defensible
answer_key.full_credit: Correctly identifies the rule as domain logic, explains why it should move, and names a domain location
answer_key.partial_credit: Correctly identifies it should move without explaining why or naming a destination

questions.prompt: A team is building a customer portal. Users can be logged-in, guests, and suspended accounts. The portal has 6 different features that behave differently for each user type. The team is considering three approaches: (1) null checks everywhere, (2) a single UserType enum with switch statements, (3) Special Case objects for Guest and Suspended. Using pressure-first reasoning and boundary-isolation principles from Module 4, evaluate all three approaches and recommend one.
questions.guidance: A strong response maps the edge-condition pressure to each approach: approach 1 scatters policy, approach 2 centralizes the type but still requires each feature to know all cases, approach 3 localizes each user type's policy in an object. The recommendation should explain what happens when a fourth user type is added (extensibility test).

rubric.A: Evaluates all three approaches with pressure mapping, identifies Special Case as the recommendation, justifies with the extensibility test and policy-localization argument
rubric.B: Evaluates two of three correctly, makes a justified recommendation with some reasoning
rubric.C: Recommends one approach without comparative evaluation of the alternatives
